abstract: In the Afrikaans-speaking South African theological tradition, Rudolf Bultmann’s work—specifically his program of demythologisation—has historically been met with significant skepticism and little positive reception. However, more than seventy years have passed since his seminal 1941 essay ignited one of the most intense theological debates of the twentieth century. While Bultmann’s legacy has receded into the background for many contemporary scholars, the 130th anniversary of his birth provides a timely opportunity to revisit his contributions. This paper aims to "dust off" Bultmann’s original 1941 thesis, seeking to listen to his voice directly, unmediated by the layers of critique and interpretation that have accumulated over the decades. Now that the fervor of the original debate has settled, a fresh engagement with his work suggests that Bultmann’s existential insights remain profoundly relevant within our current theological landscape.
